title: Age
examTip: Age affects both the very young and the very old — don't just write about elderly people. Examiners want to see you address youth inequality AND old-age inequality, with named sociologists for each.
introTips: Ageism can work both ways — young people and old people both face discrimination, just in different contexts.
The concept of the 'social construction of age' is important — what 'old' means changes across cultures and history.
The generational inequality debate (younger generations vs baby boomers) is modern and highly relevant to exam questions.
intro: Your age shapes almost everything about how society treats you — which rights you have, which spaces you're welcome in, whether employers take you seriously and how much respect you receive. Age is not just a number: it is a social category that organises power, status and life chances, and it affects people at both ends of the age spectrum in very different ways.

keyPoints.heading: Age as a Dimension of Stratification
keyPoints.content: Age, like class, gender and ethnicity, creates differences in power, status and life chances. Ageism — prejudice and discrimination based on age — was a term coined by Robert Butler (1969). Both the young and the old can face ageism, but it most severely affects older people in the labour market. The social construction of age is a key sociological idea: what it means to be 'old,' 'young' or 'middle-aged,' and how people at different life stages should behave, varies enormously across cultures and historical periods. In many societies, elders hold great authority and command deep respect; in Western consumer culture, youth is fetishised and ageing treated as something to be hidden. Age stratification refers to the way society is formally organised around age: compulsory schooling ends at 16, the age of criminal responsibility is 10 in England and Wales, retirement ages are set by the state, and many rights (voting, drinking, driving) are age-gated. These legal age boundaries are social decisions, not biological inevitabilities.
writeBullets: Ageism — prejudice and discrimination based on age (Butler, 1969 — coined the term)
Both young and old face ageism — but in different contexts and to different degrees
Social construction of age — what 'old' or 'young' means varies across cultures and history
Western consumer culture fetishises youth; many other cultures revere elders
Age stratification — society organised around age: schooling, criminal responsibility, voting, retirement
Legal age boundaries (retirement age, voting age) are social decisions, not biological facts

keyTerms.def: Prejudice and discrimination based on a person's age — coined by Robert Butler in 1969
The idea that what 'old' or 'young' means is defined by society, not biology — it varies across cultures and history
The organisation of society around age categories, giving different rights and status to different age groups
American gerontologist who coined the term 'ageism' in 1969 to describe prejudice and discrimination against older people

keyPoints.memTip: Butler 1969 = ageism coined. Age is socially constructed = what 'old' means is a social choice, not a biological fact.
keyPoints.tutor_note: Prompt students to think about how age categories are legally enforced — retirement age, criminal responsibility, voting — as evidence of age stratification.

keyPoints.heading: Youth and Society
keyPoints.content: Young people form distinctive subcultures — shared styles, values and practices — as part of the transition from childhood to adulthood. The Centre for Contemporary Cultural Studies (CCCS, Birmingham, 1970s) — associated with sociologists including Phil Cohen, Stuart Hall and Dick Hebdige — argued that youth subcultures are forms of symbolic resistance to dominant culture. Dick Hebdige (1979, 'Subculture: The Meaning of Style') showed how punk used bricolage — reusing everyday objects (safety pins, bin liners) in shocking new ways — to express resistance to unemployment and mainstream culture. Moral panics — a concept developed by Stan Cohen (1972, 'Folk Devils and Moral Panics') — describe how the media exaggerates and distorts youth deviance, creating fear disproportionate to the actual threat. Examples include: the Mods and Rockers panics of the 1960s, mugging panics in the 1970s, 'hoodies' in the 2000s, and drill music today. Cohen described a media amplification spiral: media attention → police crackdown → more arrests → more media coverage → public fear grows. Youth unemployment is consistently higher than adult unemployment and has lasting effects on mental health, identity and life chances.
writeBullets: Youth subcultures — distinctive styles and values; CCCS (Birmingham, 1970s): symbolic resistance to dominant culture
Hebdige (1979) 'Subculture: The Meaning of Style' — punk used bricolage to resist unemployment and mainstream culture
Bricolage — reusing everyday objects (safety pins, bin bags) in new, shocking ways as cultural resistance
Moral panic (Stan Cohen, 1972) — media exaggerates youth deviance, creating disproportionate public fear
Media amplification spiral: media → police crackdown → arrests → more media → more fear
Youth unemployment consistently higher than adult rate — harms mental health and life chances

keyTerms.def: A group with shared values, styles and practices that distinguish them from mainstream culture
Hebdige's term for youth subcultures' practice of taking everyday objects and using them in new, subversive ways
Stan Cohen's (1972) concept — media exaggeration of a social threat, creating public fear disproportionate to the actual danger
Cohen's model: media coverage → police response → more arrests → more coverage → escalating public fear
Stan Cohen's term for the groups (Mods, Rockers, hoodies) scapegoated during moral panics
Centre for Contemporary Cultural Studies — Birmingham university research centre that produced foundational studies of youth subcultures in the 1970s

keyPoints.memTip: Stan Cohen's moral panic: Media → Police → Arrests → Media → Fear. A spiral that feeds itself.
keyPoints.tutor_note: Moral panic is one of the most heavily tested concepts in GCSE sociology — ensure students can name Cohen, explain the amplification spiral and give a modern example.

keyPoints.heading: Old Age and Inequality
keyPoints.content: The UK has an ageing population — 19% of the population was over 65 in 2022, projected to rise to 26% by 2050 (ONS). This creates significant social challenges: pressure on the NHS, rising pension costs, and debates about the state pension age (set to rise to 68 by 2044). Despite the Employment Equality (Age) Regulations 2006 and the Equality Act 2010 outlawing age discrimination in employment, older workers continue to face discrimination in hiring and are disproportionately targeted in redundancies. Pensioner poverty is a serious issue — 18% of pensioners live in poverty after housing costs (2022), with women particularly affected due to interrupted careers and lower lifetime earnings meaning they rely heavily on the state pension. Loneliness is a major social problem: 1.4 million older people are chronically lonely (Age UK), and research by Julianne Holt-Lunstad (2015) found social isolation is as harmful to health as smoking 15 cigarettes a day. The social care crisis — characterised by inadequate government funding and severe workforce shortages — leaves many older people without appropriate support.
writeBullets: Ageing population — 19% of UK population over 65 (2022); projected 26% by 2050 (ONS)
Ageism in the workplace — older workers discriminated against in hiring and redundancy despite Equality Act 2010
Pensioner poverty — 18% of pensioners in poverty after housing costs (2022); women worst affected
Loneliness — 1.4 million older people chronically lonely (Age UK); as harmful as smoking 15 cigarettes a day (Holt-Lunstad, 2015)
Social care crisis — underfunding and workforce shortages leave many older people without support
State pension age rising to 68 by 2044 — financial pressure on older workers to stay employed longer

keyTerms.def: A population in which the proportion of older people is growing — driven by longer life expectancy and lower birth rates
The situation of pensioners living below the poverty line — affects 18% of pensioners in the UK (2022)
The shortage of adequate, funded care for elderly and disabled people in the UK due to government underfunding and workforce shortages
Psychologist whose 2015 research showed social isolation is as harmful as smoking 15 cigarettes a day

keyPoints.memTip: 19% over 65 now; 26% by 2050. 18% pensioners in poverty. 1.4 million chronically lonely. These three statistics carry exam answers.
keyPoints.tutor_note: Loneliness is a socially caused problem — emphasise that it is a sociological issue (about social isolation and policy failure) not just personal misfortune.

keyPoints.heading: Generational Inequalities
keyPoints.content: A significant contemporary debate concerns whether younger generations face greater disadvantages than their parents and grandparents did at the same age. Evidence that younger generations are disadvantaged includes: house prices now average 9 times the average salary (ONS), making homeownership unattainable for many; student debt averaging £45,000 on graduation in England; insecure employment — gig economy work and zero-hours contracts are disproportionately held by young people; and young people will bear the brunt of climate change consequences while having benefited less from post-war prosperity than the 'baby boomer' generation. The 'Bank of Mum and Dad' is now effectively the UK's 10th largest mortgage lender — but this only helps those whose parents have wealth, deepening class inequality within younger generations. Age and intersectionality compounds these disadvantages: being old AND female AND from an ethnic minority creates compounded disadvantage (Crenshaw, 1989). Postmodern perspectives argue that age identities are increasingly fluid ('60 is the new 40'), but sociologists caution that cultural representations do not eliminate material inequalities in income, wealth or health.
writeBullets: House prices — average 9× average salary (ONS); homeownership increasingly unattainable for young people
Student debt — average £45,000 on graduation in England
Insecure employment — gig economy and zero-hours contracts disproportionately affect young workers
Bank of Mum and Dad — 10th largest mortgage lender; only helps those with wealthy parents, deepening class inequality
Age intersectionality — old + female + ethnic minority = compounded disadvantage (Crenshaw, 1989)
Postmodern view — age identities more fluid ('60 is the new 40') but material inequalities persist

keyTerms.def: Differences in wealth, opportunity and disadvantage between different age cohorts or generations
An economy characterised by short-term, flexible or freelance work rather than permanent employment — often insecure and lacking benefits
An employment contract with no guaranteed hours — worker must be available but may receive no work
Colloquial term for parental financial support in house purchase — acts as a de facto mortgage lender but only benefits those with wealthy parents

keyPoints.memTip: Young people's triple squeeze: house prices 9× salary + £45k student debt + insecure gig work. All structural, not personal failure.
keyPoints.tutor_note: The generational inequality debate connects age to class — the Bank of Mum and Dad reproduces class advantage within the younger generation.

tables.title: Age Stratification: Youth vs Old Age — Power, Inequalities and Perspectives
rows: Power and status
Low formal power; age-gated rights (voting at 18, driving at 17)
Declining power in labour market; but formal state protections (pension, Equality Act)
Butler (1969): ageism affects both groups; old age = most severe labour market discrimination
Key inequality
Unemployment, insecure work, student debt, unaffordable housing
Pensioner poverty (18%), loneliness (1.4m chronically lonely), social care crisis
Generational inequality: younger generations face structural disadvantages (ONS house price data)
Cultural representation
Fetishised in consumer culture but criminalised in media (moral panics)
Marginalised or invisible in youth-obsessed media; postmodern 'ageing is fluid' discourse
Stan Cohen (1972): moral panics demonise youth; Holt-Lunstad (2015): isolation kills
Sociological theory
CCCS: subcultures as symbolic resistance; Hebdige (1979): bricolage and punk
Functionalism: retirement frees up roles for younger workers (Parsons — role exit)
Marxist: older and younger workers exploited differently by capitalism; intersectionality applies
Policy response
Youth unemployment programmes; raising school-leaving age; minimum wage
Equality Act 2010 (age discrimination); rising state pension age; social care underfunding
Debate: legal reform insufficient without tackling structural ageism in organisations

1.question: What did Stan Cohen mean by a 'moral panic'?
options: When the media exaggerates a social threat, creating public fear disproportionate to the actual danger
When young people deliberately cause widespread panic through criminal activity
When the police overreact to ordinary youth behaviour without media involvement
When a moral issue divides public opinion between older and younger generations
1.correctFeedback: Exactly right. Cohen (1972) showed how media representations of groups like Mods and Rockers created fear far greater than the actual threat — and how this spiral of coverage amplified the problem.
1.wrongFeedback: A moral panic isn't caused by young people themselves — it's a media process. Cohen (1972) described how the press exaggerates threats from groups like Mods and Rockers, creating disproportionate public fear through an amplification spiral.

2.question: What percentage of UK pensioners live in poverty after housing costs, according to 2022 data?
2.correctFeedback: Correct — 18% of pensioners live in poverty after housing costs, with women disproportionately affected due to lower lifetime earnings and reliance on the state pension.
2.wrongFeedback: The figure is 18% of pensioners in poverty after housing costs (2022). Women are hit hardest because interrupted careers mean lower lifetime earnings and greater reliance on the state pension.

commonMistakes: Only discussing older people when writing about age — examiners expect you to address youth inequality and generational inequality too.
Saying all older people are wealthy — pensioner poverty affects 18% of over-65s and is particularly severe for women.
Confusing moral panic with actual youth crime — a moral panic is about media exaggeration, not the level of real offending.
Forgetting that ageism is illegal under the Equality Act 2010 — but noting that legal protection has not eliminated discrimination.
Treating generational inequality as inevitable — it is structurally produced by housing policy, student finance, and employment law, not just demographic change.
examTips: For questions on age, structure your answer around BOTH youth AND old age — one without the other loses marks on most AQA questions.
Stan Cohen's moral panic model is one of the most heavily marked theories in the whole specification — know the amplification spiral by heart.
Use statistics to anchor your answers: 19% of the UK over 65; 18% of pensioners in poverty; 9× house price to salary ratio; 1.4 million chronically lonely older people.
Link age to intersectionality for top-band evaluation — being old AND female AND from an ethnic minority compounds disadvantage.
revisionCardBullets: Ageism — prejudice based on age; coined by Butler (1969)
Social construction of age — what 'old' means varies across cultures and history
Age stratification — rights and status tied to age (voting, retirement, criminal responsibility)
Youth subcultures — CCCS (1970s): symbolic resistance; Hebdige (1979): punk as bricolage
Moral panic (Cohen, 1972) — media exaggerates youth threat; amplification spiral
Youth unemployment consistently higher than adult rate — affects mental health and life chances
Pensioner poverty — 18% of pensioners in poverty after housing costs (2022)
Loneliness — 1.4 million older people chronically lonely (Age UK); = smoking 15 cigarettes/day (Holt-Lunstad, 2015)
House prices = 9× average salary (ONS); student debt £45,000 average on graduation
Bank of Mum and Dad — 10th largest UK mortgage lender; deepens class inequality within younger generation
Intersectionality — old + female + ethnic minority = compounded disadvantage (Crenshaw, 1989)
